  • PDF

Effect of Weather, Day of Season, and Registration Status on Bike Rentals

STAT 3302: Statistical Modeling for Discovery II

Authors

Colin Farrell

Paarth Mahajan

Aayush Sharma

Tony Zheng

Published

April 17, 2026

Abstract

Introduction

Bike rentals vary from day-to-day depending on various weather, temporal, and user conditions. By analyzing which conditions have the greatest effect on the estimated number of rentals, we can find what conditions have an impact or don’t, and how these impacts interact with each other. As such, we ask the following questions:

(RQ.1)

Does the temperature of a day affect the number of Bike Rentals after accounting for other weather conditions (e.g. precipitation, humidity, and wind speed), user type and type of day (workday/not)?

(RQ.2)

Does the fact that it is a workday (not a weekend/holiday) affect the number of Bike Rentals after accounting for weather conditions (e.g. precipitation, humidity, temperature, and wind speed), and user type?

(RQ.3)

Do weather conditions affect the number of Bike Rentals among registered users vs. casual users?

(RQ.4)

Does the fact that it is a workday (or not) affect the number of Bike Rentals among registered users vs. casual users differently?

(RQ.5)

Do the number of rentals change through a season (e.g. do rentals taper of as a season progresses)?

These questions address individual bike rental conditions and the way user they interact between each other (if one condition occurring cancels out another or strengthens its effect).

Data

The bike sharing dataset was (Fanaee-T 2013)

Methods

Model

We fit model \[ Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i) \]

with link function \[ \log(\lambda_i) = X_i^T\beta \]

for \(i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}\).

\(Y_i\) is the observed number of bike rentals for the hour of the \(i\)-th observation. For each hour, there are two \(Y\):

  • One for casual users
  • One for registered users

Then, \(X_i \in \mathbb{R}^{(p+1) \times n}\) and \(\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{p + 1}\), where - \(n\) is the number of samples in the hour dataset times two (one for each registered count, one for casual) - \(p\) is the total number of predictors we’re including, with intercept term \(\beta_0 = \beta_{0, i}\) for \(\forall i\) - \(X_{i, 1} = 1\) for \(\forall i\) - and all other \(X_{i, j}\) correspond to the value \(j\)-th predictor for observation \(i\) \(i \in {1, 2, \dots, n}\).

Predictors

Our model will include:

Predictor Description Reason
temp The temperature of a day in (\(^\circ F\)) To control for the effect of the different aspects of the weather on the number of bike rentals
windspeed The normalized wind speed from 0-67 (unitless)
hum The normalized humidity from 0-100 (unitless)
weathersit Factor representing the amount of precipitation/cloud cover. In \(\{1, 2, 3, 4\}\), where
1: Clear, Few clouds, Partly cloudy, Partly cloudy
2: Mist + Cloudy, Mist + Broken clouds, Mist + Few clouds, Mist
3: Light Snow, Light Rain + Thunderstorm + Scattered clouds, Light Rain + Scattered clouds
4: Heavy Rain + Ice Pallets + Thunderstorm + Mist, Snow + Fog		 To examine the effect of the amount of precipitation on the number of bike rentals, after accounting for other parts of the weather
User Type Indicator of if the number of rentals is taken from the casual or registered columns of the dataset (0 if casual, 1 if registered) Included to account for the effect of being a registered user by itself on rentals
season Indicator for what season it is. In the data, is an integer from 1-4, where 1 is Spring, 2 Summer, 3 Fall, and 4 Winter. For our model, we transform it into categorical data where Spr is for Spring, Sum is for Summer, Aut is for Fall, and Win is for Winter Included to account for the effect of the season on bike rentals
workingday Indicator of if day was a workday or not (1 if workday, 0 if not) Helps identify a workday and accounts for the effect of that fact on the number of rentals for the given hour
Day of season The day of the season (e.g. 0 for the first day of Spring, 1 for the second, etc…, then 0 for the first day of Fall, 1 for the second, etc…). Helps to answer RQ.5 by showing if there is a significant linear relation between how long it’s been a season and the number of rentals.
Interaction between userType and all other predictors To see how registered users are affected by each other predictor compared to casual users

We also use stepAIC (bidirectional stepwise model selection using AIC) to determine if our model would have a better fit by ablating any predictors.

Results

We get fitted model:


Call:
glm(formula = rentals ~ (temp + windspeed + hum + weathersit + 
    workingday + day_of_season + season) * user_type, family = poisson(), 
    data = bike_transformed)

Coefficients:
                                    Estimate Std. Error  z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)                        2.906e+00  8.519e-03  341.176  < 2e-16 ***
temp                               3.621e+00  1.125e-02  321.782  < 2e-16 ***
windspeed                          3.365e-01  1.097e-02   30.674  < 2e-16 ***
hum                               -2.105e+00  8.145e-03 -258.460  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit2                        1.345e-01  3.258e-03   41.295  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit3                       -1.025e-01  7.111e-03  -14.407  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit4                       -3.154e-01  3.536e-01   -0.892   0.3724    
workingday                        -8.667e-01  2.547e-03 -340.287  < 2e-16 ***
day_of_season                      1.526e-03  5.026e-05   30.366  < 2e-16 ***
seasonSum                          3.534e-01  5.628e-03   62.805  < 2e-16 ***
seasonAut                         -4.757e-02  6.565e-03   -7.246 4.28e-13 ***
seasonWin                          5.619e-01  5.233e-03  107.378  < 2e-16 ***
user_typeregistered                1.416e+00  9.352e-03  151.439  < 2e-16 ***
temp:user_typeregistered          -1.714e+00  1.239e-02 -138.311  < 2e-16 ***
windspeed:user_typeregistered     -5.115e-02  1.218e-02   -4.201 2.66e-05 ***
hum:user_typeregistered            8.173e-01  8.988e-03   90.930  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit2:user_typeregistered   -5.280e-02  3.591e-03  -14.704  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit3:user_typeregistered   -5.399e-02  7.712e-03   -7.001 2.55e-12 ***
weathersit4:user_typeregistered    7.505e-01  3.601e-01    2.084   0.0372 *  
workingday:user_typeregistered     1.139e+00  2.909e-03  391.404  < 2e-16 ***
day_of_season:user_typeregistered  8.852e-04  5.552e-05   15.943  < 2e-16 ***
seasonSum:user_typeregistered     -2.916e-01  6.119e-03  -47.652  < 2e-16 ***
seasonAut:user_typeregistered     -1.608e-02  7.161e-03   -2.246   0.0247 *  
seasonWin:user_typeregistered     -1.199e-01  5.640e-03  -21.260  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for poisson family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 4643075  on 34757  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 2177102  on 34734  degrees of freedom
AIC: 2358946

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5

Which is the same after running stepAIC (backwards + forwards stepwise AIC model selection) starting from the full model and from the null model.

Diagnostics

We can generate a diagnostic plot for the estimated log rentals against residuals (for both Pearson and deviance residuals)

It appears that our model’s residuals are fairly well-fitted for low to medium amounts of bike rentals (0 to about 200). They seem to be centered around 0 for both Pearson and deviance.

For larger values, it appears that both types of residual are biased negatively, which means that our model is sometimes overestimating the number of rentals. This means that our model might not be appropriate; possible causes might be that there is some factor that causes bike rentals to be lower in these cases, or that our predictors are not truly linear (e.g. it might be that a more accurate relationship would be that \(\log(\mathbb{E}(rentals))\) is proportional to the log of temperature, rather than the actual temperature).

Discussion

(RQ.1)

The estimated coefficients for temp are seen to be statistically significant, as the individual p-values for temp and temp:user_typeregistered are given by:

                    temp temp:user_typeregistered 
                       0                        0

with combined p-value

[1] 0

The individual values of the coefficients are

                    temp temp:user_typeregistered 
                3.621075                -1.713888

meaning for casual users, a one degree increase in temperature results in an estimated multiplicative increase in mean bike use by

[1] 37.37773

and, for casual users, a smaller yet significant estimated mean increase of

[1] 6.734118

(RQ.2)

Workdays also had an effect on the average number of rides, as the coefficient is statistically significant with associated p-value

[1] 0

and the model is statistically significant compared to the model without both workday coefficients, having p-value on the two predictors

[1] 0

The coefficient value for the workingday predictor is

workingday 
-0.8666524

Therefore, the estimated multiplicative change in mean number of riders when it is a working day compared to a non-working day, i.e., a holiday or weekend, is given by

[1] 0.4203564

which means that approximately 58% less average rides are estimated on a workday compared to a non-workday.

(RQ.5)

Our estimated day_of_season coefficients were statistically significant, since day_of_season and day_of_season:user_typeregistered had individual p-values of

                    day_of_season day_of_season:user_typeregistered 
                    1.563248e-202                      3.162649e-57

and a p-value when added together

[1] 0

with individual values

                    day_of_season day_of_season:user_typeregistered 
                     0.0015263168                      0.0008852259

These indicate that there was a significant positive relationship between the day of the season and average bike rentals. Bike rentals increased in later parts of seasons for all users - though to a slightly greater extent for registered users. Specifically, it is estimated that for every day that passed in a season, the estimated mean number of casual bike rentals in an hour increased by a factor of

[1] 1.001527

and for registered users

[1] 1.002414

Conclusion

Bike rentals are significantly associated with different climate/weather conditions. They

  • Significantly increased for higher temperatures
  • Increased for non-working days
  • Increased as a season went along (later parts of a season were associated with more rentals)

References

Fanaee-T, Hadi. 2013. “Bike Sharing.” UCI Machine Learning Repository.

Appendix

The model was fit using

model = glm(rentals ~ (temp + windspeed + hum + weathersit + workingday + day_of_season + season)*user_type, bike_transformed, family = poisson())

s = summary(model)
s
